schools.summary: The law of the bill of divorcement: 'when a man hath taken a wife, and married her, and it come to pass that she find no favour in his eyes, because he hath found some uncleanness in her: then let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give it in her hand, and send her out of his house' — the regulation — 'when a man hath taken a wife... and it come to pass that she find no favour in his eyes, because he hath found some uncleanness in her: then let him write her a bill of divorcement... and send her out of his house': the law did not institute divorce but regulated and restrained an existing practice (the witnesses noting the significance — the law tolerated and regulated divorce because of the hardness of men's hearts, not as God's original design, the bill of divorcement a check on hasty putting-away, requiring a formal writing and protecting the woman, the lesson — that this was a concession to human hardness, not the will of God, the requirement of a written bill restraining caprice, the woman not to be cast off at a word, the truth that Christ declared this a permission for the hardness of heart, not the creation ordinance, 'Moses because of the hardness of your hearts suffered you to put away your wives: but from the beginning it was not so,' Matthew 19:8, the figure — the contrast between the law's concession and the gospel's restoration of marriage to its first design, 'what therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder,' Matthew 19:6, the truth that the indissoluble union of marriage was God's original purpose, the bill of divorce a temporary accommodation, the figure of the law that restrains sin it cannot cure, the gospel restoring the ideal, the warning against the hardness of heart that breaks the marriage bond, the call back to God's design, the figure of the regulation of divorce as a concession to hardness of heart, fulfilled in the gospel's restoration of marriage); the disputed 'uncleanness,' the ground of the divorce, variously understood, the occasion of the practice the law regulated (the hardness of heart, Matthew 19:8) — the ground (some uncleanness); 'then let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give it in her hand, and send her out of his house' (the formal bill of divorcement, a restraint on hasty putting-away and a protection of the woman, the practice regulated not instituted, the concession restrained, Matthew 19:6) — the figure (the gospel restoring marriage to its first design, the providence, the law, the divorced woman not to return to follow, vv.2-4) — Keil, Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Did the bill of divorcement institute or merely regulate divorce?

sides.position: It regulated and restrained an existing practice as a concession to hardness of heart, not God's original design - the written bill checking hasty putting-away and protecting the woman; Christ declared it a permission 'because of the hardness of your hearts,' not the creation ordinance (Matthew 19:8), fulfilled in the gospel's restoration of marriage to its first indissoluble design, 'what God hath joined together, let not man put asunder' (Matthew 19:6)

themes: When a man hath taken a wife, and married her, and it come to pass that she find no favour in his eyes, because he hath found some uncleanness in her: then let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give it in her hand, and send her out of his house
the law of the bill of divorcement (the crux of the regulation, not the institution, of divorce)
the law did not institute but regulated and restrained divorce, the written bill checking hasty putting-away and protecting the woman, a concession to hardness of heart
not the creation ordinance, fulfilled in the gospel's restoration of marriage to its first indissoluble design (Matthew 19:6; 19:8)

schools.summary: That the divorced woman might marry another: 'and when she is departed out of his house, she may go and be another man's wife' — the divorced woman was free to marry another man (the and when she is departed out of his house, the departure, the she may go and be another man's wife, the remarriage, the figure, the divorced woman free to remarry, the practice the law regulated, the truth that the bill of divorce freed her to marry again, the figure of the toleration that the law allowed, the remarriage permitted, the practice the law restrained but did not forbid, the woman free, the regulation that led to the prohibition of return, vv.4); the woman departed from the house (the divorce) — the departure (out of the house); 'she may go and be another man's wife' (the divorced woman free to remarry, the toleration, the figure, the practice regulated, the providence, the remarriage, the prohibition of return to follow, vv.3-4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And when she is departed out of his house, she may go and be another man's wife
that the divorced woman might marry another
the divorced woman free to marry another, the practice the law regulated
the toleration the law allowed but did not forbid

schools.summary: The case of the second husband divorcing her or dying: 'and if the latter husband hate her, and write her a bill of divorcement, and giveth it in her hand, and sendeth her out of his house; or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife' — the case is put of the second marriage ending by divorce or death (the and if the latter husband hate her, and write her a bill of divorcement, the second divorce, the and giveth it in her hand, and sendeth her out of his house, the putting away, the or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife, or the death, the figure, the second marriage ending, the case that leads to the prohibition, the truth that whether by divorce or death, the second union ending, the woman might not return to the first, the figure of the case defined, the ground of the prohibition to follow, the second husband's divorce or death, the woman free again, the case that grounds the law of vv.4); the second husband divorcing her (the putting away) — the case (the second divorce); 'or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife' (the second husband's death, the union ended, the figure, the case grounding the prohibition, the providence, the case, the prohibition of return to the first husband to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if the latter husband hate her, and write her a bill of divorcement, and giveth it in her hand, and sendeth her out of his house; or if the latter husband die, which took her to be his wife
the case of the second husband divorcing her or dying
the second marriage ending by divorce or death
the case that grounds the prohibition against returning to the first husband

schools.summary: That the first husband might not take her again after she was defiled: 'her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled; for that is abomination before the LORD: and thou shalt not cause the land to sin, which the LORD thy God giveth thee for an inheritance' — the first husband was forbidden to remarry the woman after her second marriage (the her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, the prohibition of return, the after that she is defiled, after the second marriage, the for that is abomination before the LORD, the abomination, the and thou shalt not cause the land to sin, the land not to be defiled, the figure, the prohibition of return, the marriage not to be lightly broken and resumed, the truth that the bond once broken and another formed could not be resumed, lest marriage be made light of, the figure of the restraint on hasty divorce, the prohibition a check on caprice, the dignity of marriage guarded, the land not to be defiled, the abomination that this restraint forbade, Jeremiah 3:1); the first husband forbidden to remarry her (the abomination, Jeremiah 3:1) — the prohibition (no return); 'thou shalt not cause the land to sin' (the land not to be defiled by this sin, the dignity of marriage guarded, the figure, the restraint on hasty divorce, the providence, the prohibition, the newly married man exempt from war to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled; for that is abomination before the LORD: and thou shalt not cause the land to sin, which the LORD thy God giveth thee for an inheritance
that the first husband might not take her again after she was defiled
the first husband forbidden to remarry the woman after her second marriage, a check on caprice that guarded the dignity of marriage
the land not to be defiled (Jeremiah 3:1)

schools.summary: The newly married man free from war for a year to cheer his wife: 'when a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war, neither shall he be charged with any business: but he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife which he hath taken' — the newly married man was exempt from war and public duty for a year (the when a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war, exempt from war, the neither shall he be charged with any business, free of public duty, the but he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife which he hath taken, free to gladden his wife, the figure, the kindness of the law to the newly married, the first year free to establish the home, the truth that the law cherished the marriage bond, the husband free to gladden his wife, the figure of the tenderness of God's law toward marriage and the home, the joy of the first year guarded, the care for the new household, the wife to be cheered, the gentleness of the law that values the home, vv.4); the newly married man exempt from war and duty (the kindness) — the exemption (free from war); 'he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife' (the first year free to gladden his wife, the care for the home, the figure, the tenderness toward marriage, the providence, the exemption, the millstone not to be taken in pledge to follow, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war, neither shall he be charged with any business: but he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife which he hath taken

the newly married man exempt from war and duty for a year to gladden his wife
the kindness of the law that cherished the marriage bond and the joy of the first year

schools.summary: That the millstone was not to be taken in pledge: 'no man shall take the nether or the upper millstone to pledge: for he taketh a man's life to pledge' — the millstone, by which the family ground their daily bread, was not to be taken as a pledge (the no man shall take the nether or the upper millstone to pledge, the millstone not taken, the for he taketh a man's life to pledge, the means of life, the figure, the prohibition of taking the means of livelihood in pledge, the mercy of the law, the truth that to take the millstone was to take the family's daily bread, their very life, the figure of the law's care for the poor, the necessities of life not to be seized, the mercy that forbids depriving a man of his means of living, the protection of the household's sustenance, vv.10-13); the millstone not to be taken as a pledge (the mercy) — the prohibition (the millstone); 'he taketh a man's life to pledge' (the means of life not to be seized, the care for the poor, the figure, the necessities of life protected, the providence, the prohibition, the law against man-stealing to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The man-stealer to be put to death: 'if a man be found stealing any of his brethren of the children of Israel, and maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him; then that thief shall die; and thou shalt put evil away from among you' — the man-stealer, who kidnapped and sold a fellow Israelite, was to die (the if a man be found stealing any of his brethren of the children of Israel, the kidnapping, the and maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him, the selling, the then that thief shall die; and thou shalt put evil away from among you, the death and purging, the figure, the man-stealer judged with death, the gravity of the crime, the truth that to steal and sell a man was a capital crime, the evil purged, the figure of the sanctity of human freedom, the kidnapper condemned, 'menstealers' among the lawless, 1 Timothy 1:10, the gravity of trafficking in men, the death penalty, the evil rooted out, the freedom of the person guarded, vv.6); the kidnapping and selling of a man (the crime, 1 Timothy 1:10) — the crime (man-stealing); 'then that thief shall die; and thou shalt put evil away from among you' (the man-stealer put to death, the evil purged, the sanctity of freedom, the figure, the kidnapper condemned, 1 Timothy 1:10, the providence, the judgment, the law of leprosy to follow, vv.8-9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The charge to observe the law of leprosy as the priests taught: 'take heed in the plague of leprosy, that thou observe diligently, and do according to all that the priests the Levites shall teach you: as I commanded them, so ye shall observe to do' — the people were to observe carefully the law of leprosy as the priests instructed (the take heed in the plague of leprosy, that thou observe diligently, the diligent heed, the and do according to all that the priests the Levites shall teach you, as the priests teach, the as I commanded them, so ye shall observe to do, by God's command, the figure, the careful observance of the law of leprosy, the priests' instruction followed, the truth that the law of cleansing was to be kept exactly, the priests the appointed teachers, the figure of submission to the appointed ministry in the matters of God, the priests' word obeyed, the diligence in the matters of purity, the leprosy a figure of sin, the ordinance kept as God commanded, vv.9); the diligent heed to the law of leprosy (the observance) — the heed (diligent); 'do according to all that the priests the Levites shall teach you' (the priests' instruction followed, submission to the ministry, the figure, the ordinance kept as God commanded, the providence, the charge, the remembrance of Miriam to follow, vv.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The call to remember Miriam's leprosy: 'remember what the LORD thy God did unto Miriam by the way, after that ye were come forth out of Egypt' — the people were to remember how God struck Miriam with leprosy for her sin (the remember what the LORD thy God did unto Miriam by the way, the remembrance of Miriam, the after that ye were come forth out of Egypt, in the wilderness, the figure, the example of Miriam, struck with leprosy for her rebellion, a warning, the truth that even Miriam was not spared, the leprosy a judgment on sin, the figure of the judgment on murmuring and pride, Miriam the example, Numbers 12:10, the warning that leprosy was a mark of God's displeasure, the call to heed the law, the remembrance that enforces the law of leprosy, vv.8); the call to remember Miriam's leprosy (the warning, Numbers 12:10) — the remembrance (of Miriam); 'after that ye were come forth out of Egypt' (the judgment in the wilderness, the example of Miriam, the figure, the judgment on sin remembered, the providence, the remembrance, the law of pledges to follow, vv.10-13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That the lender was not to enter the house to take the pledge: 'when thou dost lend thy brother any thing, thou shalt not go into his house to fetch his pledge' — the lender was not to invade the borrower's house to seize the pledge (the when thou dost lend thy brother any thing, the loan, the thou shalt not go into his house to fetch his pledge, not entering to seize, the figure, the respect for the borrower's home and dignity, the lender not to invade, the truth that the poor man's house and dignity were to be respected, the pledge not seized within, the figure of the consideration for the debtor's dignity, the lender to wait outside, the kindness that does not humiliate the poor, the home respected, the gentleness of the law toward the debtor, vv.11); the loan to a brother (the lending) — the loan (to a brother); 'thou shalt not go into his house to fetch his pledge' (the lender not to invade the house, the dignity of the poor, the figure, the consideration for the debtor, the providence, the law, the lender to stand outside to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That the lender was to stand outside and the borrower bring the pledge: 'thou shalt stand abroad, and the man to whom thou dost lend shall bring out the pledge abroad unto thee' — the lender was to wait outside and let the borrower bring the pledge himself (the thou shalt stand abroad, the lender outside, the and the man to whom thou dost lend shall bring out the pledge abroad unto thee, the borrower bringing it, the figure, the lender waiting outside, the borrower's dignity preserved, the truth that the poor man was to be spared the indignity of seizure within his home, the figure of the gentleness that respects the debtor, the pledge brought out freely, the consideration that does not shame the poor, the home unviolated, the kindness in the manner of taking the pledge, vv.10); the lender to wait outside (the respect) — the manner (stand outside); 'the man to whom thou dost lend shall bring out the pledge abroad unto thee' (the borrower bringing the pledge himself, the dignity preserved, the figure, the gentleness that respects the debtor, the providence, the manner, the pledge of the poor restored at sundown to follow, vv.12-13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That the poor man's pledge was not to be kept overnight: 'and if the man be poor, thou shalt not sleep with his pledge' — the pledge of a poor man, often his garment, was not to be kept overnight (the and if the man be poor, the poor man, the thou shalt not sleep with his pledge, not kept overnight, the figure, the mercy to the poor, his pledge not retained overnight, the truth that the poor man's garment, his covering by night, was to be returned, the figure of the compassion that will not deprive the poor of his necessity, the pledge restored that the poor not lack his covering, the kindness that values the poor man's need, vv.13); the case of the poor man (the mercy) — the poor (the man); 'thou shalt not sleep with his pledge' (the pledge not kept overnight, the compassion for the poor, the figure, the poor not deprived of his necessity, the providence, the mercy, the pledge restored at sundown to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The pledge restored at sundown, a righteousness before God: 'in any case thou shalt deliver him the pledge again when the sun goeth down, that he may sleep in his own raiment, and bless thee: and it shall be righteousness unto thee before the LORD thy God' — the poor man's garment was to be returned by night that he sleep warm and bless the lender (the in any case thou shalt deliver him the pledge again when the sun goeth down, the pledge restored at sundown, the that he may sleep in his own raiment, and bless thee, the poor blessing the lender, the and it shall be righteousness unto thee before the LORD thy God, the righteousness reckoned, the figure, the mercy that restores the poor man's covering, reckoned as righteousness, the truth that kindness to the poor is a righteousness before God, the blessing of the poor a reward, the figure of the works of mercy that God accepts, the poor's blessing and God's approval, 'blessed is he that considereth the poor,' Psalm 41:1, the compassion that God counts as righteousness, the garment restored, the kindness rewarded by God and the poor, vv.12); the garment returned that the poor sleep warm and bless the lender (the mercy, Psalm 41:1) — the restoration (at sundown); 'it shall be righteousness unto thee before the LORD thy God' (the kindness reckoned as righteousness, the works of mercy God accepts, the figure, the compassion God counts as righteousness, the providence, the righteousness, the wages of the poor not to be withheld to follow, vv.14-15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The charge not to oppress the poor hired servant: 'thou shalt not oppress an hired servant that is poor and needy, whether he be of thy brethren, or of thy strangers that are in thy land within thy gates' — the poor labourer, whether Israelite or stranger, was not to be oppressed (the thou shalt not oppress an hired servant that is poor and needy, the poor servant not oppressed, the whether he be of thy brethren, or of thy strangers that are in thy land within thy gates, Israelite or stranger, the figure, the protection of the poor labourer from oppression, the care for the weak, the truth that the hired servant, dependent on his wages, was not to be wronged, brother or stranger, the figure of the justice that protects the poor worker, no oppression, the equal care for the stranger as for the brother, the poor guarded, the mercy that forbids oppressing the dependent, vv.15); the poor servant not to be oppressed (the protection) — the charge (no oppression); 'whether he be of thy brethren, or of thy strangers' (brother or stranger alike protected, the equal care, the figure, the justice that protects the poor worker, the providence, the charge, the wages paid the same day to follow, vv.15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: That the poor man's wages were to be paid the same day: 'at his day thou shalt give him his hire, neither shall the sun go down upon it; for he is poor, and setteth his heart upon it: lest he cry against thee unto the LORD, and it be sin unto thee' — the poor labourer's wages were to be paid the same day, for he depended on them (the at his day thou shalt give him his hire, the wages paid that day, the neither shall the sun go down upon it, not held overnight, the for he is poor, and setteth his heart upon it, the poor depending on it, the lest he cry against thee unto the LORD, and it be sin unto thee, the cry of the oppressed, the figure, the prompt payment of the poor man's wages, the care for his need, the truth that to withhold the labourer's wages was oppression, the cry heard by God, 'the hire of the labourers... which is of you kept back by fraud, crieth,' James 5:4, the figure of the justice that pays the poor promptly, the wages not withheld, the cry of the oppressed that God hears, the sin of withholding, the mercy that meets the poor man's daily need, vv.14); the poor man's wages paid the same day (the care, James 5:4) — the wages (paid promptly); 'lest he cry against thee unto the LORD, and it be sin unto thee' (the cry of the oppressed heard by God, the sin of withholding, James 5:4, the figure, God hears the cry of the poor, the providence, the wages, individual responsibility in judgment to follow, vv.16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The law that each man is to die for his own sin, not the fathers for the children nor the children for the fathers: 'the fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers: every man shall be put to death for his own sin' — individual responsibility — 'the fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers: every man shall be put to death for his own sin': in human justice, none was to be executed for another's crime, but each for his own (the witnesses noting the significance — the principle of individual responsibility in the administration of justice, the judge not to punish the innocent for the guilty, each to bear his own guilt before the law, the lesson — that justice is personal, the guilty alone to suffer, the innocent not punished for another, the magistrate bound to this rule, no vicarious punishment in human courts, the truth that this principle, here given to the judges, was taken up by the prophets against the proverb that the children's teeth were set on edge by the fathers' grapes, 'the soul that sinneth, it shall die,' Ezekiel 18:20, 'every one shall die for his own iniquity,' Jeremiah 31:30, the figure — the personal accountability of each soul before God, every man to give account of himself, 'so then every one of us shall give account of himself to God,' Romans 14:12, the truth that God judges each according to his own works, the guilt not transferred, the figure of the justice that holds each responsible, against the notion of inherited punishment, the distinction between this rule for human courts and the gospel's one exception, the willing substitution of Christ who bore the sins of others, the figure of individual responsibility, each man answerable for his own sin); none to be executed for another's crime, the innocent not punished for the guilty (the personal justice, Ezekiel 18:20) — the rule (not one for another); 'every man shall be put to death for his own sin' (each to bear his own guilt, the principle taken up by the prophets and apostles, every man to give account of himself, the individual responsibility, Jeremiah 31:30; Romans 14:12) — the figure (the personal accountability of each soul before God, the providence, the rule, justice to the stranger and fatherless to follow, vv.17-18) — Keil, Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'every man shall be put to death for his own sin' establish?

sides.position: Individual responsibility in judgment - in human justice none is to be executed for another's crime, but each to bear his own guilt, the innocent not punished for the guilty; a principle taken up by the prophets against inherited punishment (Ezekiel 18:20; Jeremiah 31:30) and by the apostle in the personal accountability of every soul before God (Romans 14:12), distinct from the gospel's one willing exception, Christ bearing the sins of others

themes: The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers: every man shall be put to death for his own sin
the law that each man is to die for his own sin (the crux of individual responsibility in judgment)
the principle of personal justice in the courts, the innocent not punished for the guilty, each to bear his own guilt
taken up by the prophets against inherited punishment and by the apostle in the personal accountability of every soul before God (Ezekiel 18:20; Jeremiah 31:30; Romans 14:12)

schools.summary: The charge not to pervert the justice of the stranger, fatherless, and widow: 'thou shalt not pervert the judgment of the stranger, nor of the fatherless; nor take a widow's raiment to pledge' — the justice of the weak was not to be perverted, nor the widow's garment taken in pledge (the thou shalt not pervert the judgment of the stranger, nor of the fatherless, no perverting their justice, the nor take a widow's raiment to pledge, the widow's garment spared, the figure, the special protection of the weak and defenceless, the stranger, fatherless, and widow, the truth that those without earthly defenders were under God's special care, their justice guarded, the figure of the justice that protects the helpless, 'pure religion... to visit the fatherless and widows,' James 1:27, the care that does not deprive the widow even of her garment, the mercy that defends the defenceless, vv.18); the justice of the stranger and fatherless not to be perverted (the protection, James 1:27) — the justice (not perverted); 'nor take a widow's raiment to pledge' (the widow's garment not taken, the care for the defenceless, the figure, the justice that protects the helpless, the providence, the charge, the remembrance of bondage in Egypt to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Thou shalt not pervert the judgment of the stranger, nor of the fatherless; nor take a widow's raiment to pledge

schools.summary: The remembrance of bondage in Egypt as the motive for mercy: 'but thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt, and the LORD thy God redeemed thee thence: therefore I command thee to do this thing' — the memory of their own bondage and redemption was the motive for mercy to the weak (the but thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt, the memory of bondage, the and the LORD thy God redeemed thee thence, the redemption, the therefore I command thee to do this thing, the motive for mercy, the figure, the remembrance of their own slavery as the ground of compassion, mercy because they were once helpless, the truth that those who were once oppressed and redeemed should show mercy to the oppressed, the figure of the gratitude that breeds compassion, the redeemed merciful to the weak, the memory of God's mercy the spring of mercy to others, 'freely ye have received, freely give,' Matthew 10:8, the redemption that grounds the duty of mercy, vv.17); the memory of bondage and redemption (the motive, Matthew 10:8) — the remembrance (a bondman); 'therefore I command thee to do this thing' (the duty of mercy grounded on the remembrance, the gratitude that breeds compassion, the figure, the redeemed merciful to the weak, the providence, the motive, the gleaning left for the poor to follow, vv.19-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt, and the LORD thy God redeemed thee thence: therefore I command thee to do this thing

the memory of their own slavery and redemption the motive for mercy to the weak
those once oppressed and redeemed to show mercy to the oppressed (Matthew 10:8)

schools.summary: The law of leaving the forgotten sheaf and gleaning for the poor: 'when thou cuttest down thine harvest in thy field, and hast forgot a sheaf in the field, thou shalt not go again to fetch it: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow: that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all the work of thine hands' — the gleaning for the poor — 'when thou cuttest down thine harvest in thy field, and hast forgot a sheaf in the field, thou shalt not go again to fetch it: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow: that the LORD thy God may bless thee': the forgotten sheaf was to be left for the poor (the witnesses noting the significance — the provision of charity for the needy and defenceless built into the harvest law, the gleaning and the forgotten sheaf left for the stranger, fatherless, and widow, God's care for the poor woven into the law of the land, the lesson — that the harvest was not to be wholly gathered, but a portion left for the poor as their right, not the owner's bounty, the open hand that leaves something for the needy, the charity that God commands and rewards, the truth that God cares for the poor and the stranger, and commands his people to provide for them, 'thou shalt leave them for the poor and stranger: I am the LORD your God,' Leviticus 19:9-10, the figure — the generosity that God blesses, the open hand rewarded, 'he that hath pity upon the poor lendeth unto the LORD,' Proverbs 19:17, the truth that mercy to the poor brings God's blessing on all the work of the hands, the charity a means of blessing, the figure of the gospel's care for the poor, the law of love that leaves room for the needy, the very law under which Ruth gleaned and was blessed, the warning against the greed that gathers all and leaves nothing, against God's care for the poor, the figure of charity to the needy and God's care for the stranger, fatherless, and widow); the forgotten sheaf not to be fetched, left in the field (the charity in the harvest, Leviticus 19:9-10) — the law (leave the sheaf); 'it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow: that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all the work of thine hands' (the sheaf left for the stranger, fatherless, and widow, the charity that God rewards with blessing, the open hand blessed, Proverbs 19:17) — the figure (the generosity God blesses, the law of love for the poor, the providence, the gleaning, the olive and the vine to follow, vv.20-21) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why was the forgotten sheaf to be left for the poor?

sides.position: As charity to the needy and God's care for the defenceless built into the harvest law - the forgotten sheaf and gleaning left for the stranger, fatherless, and widow as their right, not the owner's bounty (Leviticus 19:9-10), the open hand that God rewards with blessing on all the work of the hands (Proverbs 19:17), the very law under which Ruth gleaned and was blessed

themes: When thou cuttest down thine harvest in thy field, and hast forgot a sheaf in the field, thou shalt not go again to fetch it: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow: that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all the work of thine hands
the law of leaving the forgotten sheaf and gleaning for the poor (the crux of charity to the needy and God's care for the stranger, fatherless, and widow)
the provision for the defenceless built into the harvest law, the forgotten sheaf left as the poor's right, God's care woven into the law
the open hand that God blesses in all the work of the hands, the very law under which Ruth gleaned (Leviticus 19:9-10; Proverbs 19:17)

schools.summary: The olive boughs not gone over again, left for the poor: 'when thou beatest thine olive tree, thou shalt not go over the boughs again: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow' — the olives left after the first beating were to be left for the poor (the when thou beatest thine olive tree, thou shalt not go over the boughs again, not beaten twice, the it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow, left for the poor, the figure, the gleaning of the olive left for the poor, the charity in the harvest, the truth that the second gathering was the poor's, not the owner's, the figure of the open hand that leaves the gleaning for the needy, the olive for the poor, the care for the defenceless in the olive harvest, vv.19, the charity woven into the law of the land); the olive boughs not beaten twice (the gleaning) — the gleaning (not gone over again); 'it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow' (the olive left for the poor, the charity, the figure, the open hand for the needy, the providence, the charity, the same for the vine to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When thou beatest thine olive tree, thou shalt not go over the boughs again: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow
the olive boughs not gone over again, left for the poor
the olives left after the first beating left for the poor, the second gathering their right
the charity woven into the law of the land

schools.summary: The vineyard not gleaned again, left for the poor: 'when thou gatherest the grapes of thy vineyard, thou shalt not glean it afterward: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow' — the grapes left after the first gathering were to be left for the poor (the when thou gatherest the grapes of thy vineyard, thou shalt not glean it afterward, not gleaned twice, the it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow, left for the poor, the figure, the gleaning of the vine left for the poor, the charity in the harvest, the truth that the second gathering was the poor's, not the owner's, the figure of the open hand that leaves the gleaning for the needy, the grapes for the poor, the care for the defenceless in the vintage, vv.19-20, the charity woven into the law of the land); the vineyard not gleaned twice (the gleaning) — the gleaning (not afterward); 'it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow' (the grapes left for the poor, the charity, the figure, the open hand for the needy, the providence, the charity, the remembrance of bondage to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When thou gatherest the grapes of thy vineyard, thou shalt not glean it afterward: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow
the vineyard not gleaned again, left for the poor
the grapes left after the first gathering left for the poor, the second gathering their right
the charity woven into the law of the land

schools.summary: The remembrance of bondage as the motive for charity: 'and thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt: therefore I command thee to do this thing' — the memory of their bondage was the ground of the charity commanded (the and thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt, the memory of bondage, the therefore I command thee to do this thing, the motive for charity, the figure, the remembrance of their own slavery the spring of charity to the poor, the truth that those once in bondage should remember the poor and stranger, the figure of the gratitude that breeds generosity, the redeemed merciful, the memory of God's mercy the motive for mercy to others, vv.18, the charity grounded on the remembrance of their own need); the memory of bondage (the motive) — the remembrance (a bondman); 'therefore I command thee to do this thing' (the charity grounded on the remembrance, the gratitude that breeds generosity, the figure, the redeemed merciful to the poor, the providence, the motive, the law of stripes to follow, ch.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt: therefore I command thee to do this thing
the remembrance of bondage as the motive for charity
the memory of their own slavery the ground of the charity commanded
those once in bondage to remember the poor and stranger
